Military unit

**Brigade of Gurkhas** is the collective name which refers to all the units in the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") that are composed of [Nepalese Gurkha](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") soldiers.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The brigade draws its heritage from Gurkha units that originally served in the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") prior to Indian independence, and prior to that served for the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The brigade includes infantry, engineering, signal, logistic and training and support units. They are known for their [khukuri](/wiki/Khukuri "Khukuri"), a distinctive heavy knife with a curved blade, and have a reputation for being fierce and brave soldiers.

The brigade celebrated 200 years of service in the British Army in 2015.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army"), [Gurkha regiments history](/wiki/Gorkha_regiments_(India) "Gorkha regiments (India)"), [Royal Gurkha Rifles history](/wiki/Royal_Gurkha_Rifles#History "Royal Gurkha Rifles"), and [Gurkhas](/wiki/Gurkhas "Gurkhas")

During the [war in Nepal](/wiki/The_Gurkha_War "The Gurkha War") in 1814, in which the British failed to annex [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") into the Empire, Army officers were shocked by the tenacity of the Gurkha soldiers and encouraged them to volunteer for the [East India Company](/wiki/Honourable_East_India_Company "Honourable East India Company"). Gurkhas served as troops of the Company in the [Pindaree War](/wiki/Pindaree_War "Pindaree War") of 1817, in [Bharatpur, Nepal](/wiki/Bharatpur,_Nepal "Bharatpur, Nepal") in 1826, and the [First](/wiki/First_Anglo-Sikh_War "First Anglo-Sikh War") and [Second](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") Sikh Wars in 1846 and 1848. During the [Sepoy Mutiny](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") in 1857, the Gurkha [regiments](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") remained loyal to the British, and became part of the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") on its formation. The [2nd Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/2nd_Gurkha_Rifles "2nd Gurkha Rifles") (The Sirmoor Rifles) and the [60th Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Green_Jackets "Royal Green Jackets") famously defended [Hindu Rao](/wiki/Raja_Hindu_Rao "Raja Hindu Rao")'s house.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Gurkhas_advancing_with_Lee_tanks_to_clear_the_Japanese_from_Imphal-Kohima_road.jpg/250px-Gurkhas_advancing_with_Lee_tanks_to_clear_the_Japanese_from_Imphal-Kohima_road.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gurkhas_advancing_with_Lee_tanks_to_clear_the_Japanese_from_Imphal-Kohima_road.jpg)

Gurkhas advancing with [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") to clear the [Japanese](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") from the Imphal–Kohima road

During the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency") in the late 1940s, Gurkhas fought as jungle soldiers as they had done in [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Training Depot Brigade of Gurkhas was established on 15 August 1951 at [Sungai Petani](/wiki/Sungai_Petani "Sungai Petani"), [Kedah](/wiki/Kedah "Kedah"), [Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya").[[9]](#cite_note-scmp-9) After the conflict ended, the Gurkhas were transferred to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), where they carried out security duties.[[9]](#cite_note-scmp-9) The troops patrolled the border checking for illegal immigrants entering the territory, most crucially during the turbulence of the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"). They were deployed to contain crowds during the [Star Ferry riots of 1966](/wiki/Hong_Kong_1966_riots "Hong Kong 1966 riots").

After Indian independence and [partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, under the [Tripartite Agreement](/wiki/Britain-India-Nepal_Tripartite_Agreement "Britain-India-Nepal Tripartite Agreement"), six Gurkha regiments joined the post-independence [Indian Army](/wiki/Indian_Army "Indian Army"). Four Gurkha regiments, the [2nd](/wiki/2nd_Gurkha_Rifles "2nd Gurkha Rifles"), [6th](/wiki/6th_Gurkha_Rifles "6th Gurkha Rifles"), [7th](/wiki/7th_Gurkha_Rifles "7th Gurkha Rifles"), and [10th Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/10th_Gurkha_Rifles "10th Gurkha Rifles"), joined the British Army on 1 January 1948.[[10]](#cite_note-research-10) The [1st/2nd Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/2nd_King_Edward_VII%27s_Own_Gurkha_Rifles_(The_Sirmoor_Rifles) "2nd King Edward VII's Own Gurkha Rifles (The Sirmoor Rifles)") was deployed to [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") at the outbreak of the [Brunei Revolt](/wiki/Brunei_Revolt "Brunei Revolt") in 1962.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 1974 [Turkey invaded Cyprus](/wiki/Turkish_invasion_of_Cyprus "Turkish invasion of Cyprus") and the 10th Gurkha Rifles was sent to defend the British sovereign base area of [Dhekelia](/wiki/Dhekelia_Cantonment "Dhekelia Cantonment").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1982, during the [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War"), the 7th Gurkha Rifles formed part of 5 Infantry Brigade and was responsible for capturing Mount William shortly before the Argentinian surrender.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

On 1 July 1994, the four rifle regiments were merged into one, the [Royal Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Gurkha_Rifles "Royal Gurkha Rifles"), and the three corps regiments (the Gurkha Military Police having been disbanded in 1965) were reduced to [squadron](/wiki/Squadron_(army) "Squadron (army)") strength. On 1 July 1997, the British government [transferred sovereignty over Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") to the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China"), which led to the closing of the local British garrison. Gurkha HQ and recruit training were moved to the UK.[[9]](#cite_note-scmp-9) The Royal Gurkha Rifles took part in operations in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") in 1999, in [UN peacekeeping operations](/wiki/UN_peacekeeping_operations "UN peacekeeping operations") in [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor") in 2000 and in [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone") later that year.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Gurkhas_exercise_DM-SD-98-00170.jpg/250px-Gurkhas_exercise_DM-SD-98-00170.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gurkhas_exercise_DM-SD-98-00170.jpg)

Gurkhas undergoing an urban warfare exercise in the United States. Note the [kukri](/wiki/Kukri "Kukri") on the webbing of the nearest soldier

In September 2008, the High Court in London ruled that the British Government must issue clear guidance on the criteria against which Gurkhas may be considered for settlement rights in the UK. On 21 May 2009, and following a lengthy [campaign by Gurkha veterans](/wiki/Gurkha_Justice_Campaign "Gurkha Justice Campaign"), the British [Home Secretary](/wiki/Home_Secretary "Home Secretary"), [Jacqui Smith](/wiki/Jacqui_Smith "Jacqui Smith"), announced that all Gurkha veterans who had served four years or more in the British Army before 1997 would be allowed to settle in Britain.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## British Gurkha units 1947–1994

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: British Gurkha units 1947–1994")]

Former units included:[[10]](#cite_note-research-10)

* [2nd King Edward VII's Own Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/2nd_King_Edward_VII%27s_Own_Gurkha_Rifles_(The_Sirmoor_Rifles) "2nd King Edward VII's Own Gurkha Rifles (The Sirmoor Rifles)") (1947–1994)
* [6th Queen Elizabeth's Own Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/6th_Queen_Elizabeth%27s_Own_Gurkha_Rifles "6th Queen Elizabeth's Own Gurkha Rifles") (1947–1994)
* [7th Duke of Edinburgh's Own Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/7th_Duke_of_Edinburgh%27s_Own_Gurkha_Rifles "7th Duke of Edinburgh's Own Gurkha Rifles") (1947–1994)

In 2018, the UK Government announced that it intended to expand the brigade by more than 800 posts, with the Queen's Gurkha Engineers receiving an additional squadron, while the Queen’s Gurkha Signals and the Queen's Own Gurkha Logistic Regiment will receive two new squadrons. Additionally, approximately 300 new posts within the Royal Gurkha Rifles will be created forming a new battalion planned for the Specialist Infantry role.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) In 2018, it was announced that from 2020, female applicants would be able to apply to join the Gurkhas.[[27]](#cite_note-28) However, this decision was reversed in 2019.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

In April 2025, the UK government announced a further increase by creating a new artillery regiment within the brigade, bearing the name the King's Gurkha Artillery. As with other Gurkha units affiliated to the support arms and services, the King's Gurkha Artillery will have its own unique cap badge combining elements that illustrate its Gurkha heritage and the heritage of its parent unit, in this case the [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery"). The creation of the King's Gurkha Artillery will mark the first time that Gurkhas have served in their own dedicated [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") unit in the British Army.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[b]](#cite_note-33)

## Recruitment and basic training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Recruitment and basic training")]

The selection process for the Gurkhas is demanding: in 2017, 230 trainee riflemen were recruited from about 25,000 applicants.[[32]](#cite_note-34) Recruitment is run by [British Gurkhas Nepal](/wiki/British_Gurkhas_Nepal "British Gurkhas Nepal"); based at [Jawalakhel](/wiki/Jawalakhel "Jawalakhel"), near [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu"), the main recruiting centre is in the city of [Pokhara](/wiki/Pokhara "Pokhara"). Recruitment sees the prospective soldier undergo two stages of selection; first the regional selection at either Pokhara or [Dharan](/wiki/Dharan "Dharan"), where the recruit undertakes a series of physical tests, written [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [numeracy](/wiki/Numeracy "Numeracy") assessments, and an [interview](/wiki/Interview "Interview").[[33]](#cite_note-35) Those that pass regional selection move forward to the central selection process in Kathmandu, which sees further physical and language tests, a medical and a second interview.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

Training for Gurkhas is conducted at the [Infantry Training Centre](/wiki/Infantry_Training_Centre_(British_Army) "Infantry Training Centre (British Army)") at [Catterick Garrison](/wiki/Catterick_Garrison "Catterick Garrison") in [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire"). It lasts for 36 weeks and addresses a range of areas such as the Brigade ethos, language training, cultural training, career management and trade selection, as well as the same 26-week Combat Infantryman's Course that the Line Infantry receive. This enables the trained Gurkha soldiers to fulfill their roles on operations and continue the traditions of their forefathers.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

In addition to running recruitment of soldiers to join the British Army, British Gurkhas Nepal also runs the recruitment process for the [Gurkha Contingent](/wiki/Gurkha_Contingent "Gurkha Contingent") of the [Singapore Police Force](/wiki/Singapore_Police_Force "Singapore Police Force"). Recruits indicate at the registration stage whether they wish to join the Singapore Police or the British Army.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Although all enlisted Gurkha soldiers are recruited from Nepalese citizens in Nepal, officers in the Brigade are either selected from those who have distinguished themselves in the ranks, or are commissioned from British or Commonwealth cadets at the [Royal Military Academy Sandhurst](/wiki/Royal_Military_Academy_Sandhurst "Royal Military Academy Sandhurst"). Officers from Sandhurst attend an intensive language course at Pokhara Camp to enable them to converse with their soldiers in the [Nepali language](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language"), although Gurkha soldiers are also expected to learn English.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

## Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Organisation")]

Brigade HQ is based at the former [Staff College, Camberley](/wiki/Staff_College,_Camberley "Staff College, Camberley"). Two battalions of the [Royal Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Gurkha_Rifles "Royal Gurkha Rifles") are formed as light role infantry; they are not equipped with either armoured or wheeled vehicles.[[38]](#cite_note-40) The 1st Battalion, Royal Gurkha Rifles is based at the [British garrison](/wiki/British_Military_Garrison_Brunei "British Military Garrison Brunei") in [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") as part of Britain's commitment to maintaining a military presence in SE [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[39]](#cite_note-41) The 2nd Battalion, Royal Gurkha Rifles is based at [Shorncliffe Army Camp](/wiki/Shorncliffe_Army_Camp "Shorncliffe Army Camp"), near [Folkestone](/wiki/Folkestone "Folkestone") in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent") as part of 16 Air Assault Brigade, and is available for deployment to most areas in Europe and Africa. The 3rd Battalion, to be stationed at [Aldershot](/wiki/Aldershot_Garrison "Aldershot Garrison"), is approximately half the size of the others, and is a so-called 'specialised infantry' battalion, serving in the [training role](/wiki/Military_advisor "Military advisor") as part of the [Specialised Infantry Group](/wiki/Specialised_Infantry_Group "Specialised Infantry Group").

## Commemoration of service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Commemoration of service")]

### London memorial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: London memorial")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Gurkha_Soldier_Monument%2C_London_-_April_2008.jpg/250px-Gurkha_Soldier_Monument%2C_London_-_April_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gurkha_Soldier_Monument,_London_-_April_2008.jpg)

A monument to the Gurkha Soldier near the Ministry of Defence in London

The [British memorial to the Gurkhas](/wiki/Gurkha_Memorial,_London "Gurkha Memorial, London") was unveiled by Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II_of_the_United_Kingdom "Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom") on 3 December 1997. The inscription on the monument is a quotation from Sir [Ralph Lilley Turner](/wiki/Ralph_Lilley_Turner "Ralph Lilley Turner"), a former officer in the [3rd Gurkha Rifles](/wiki/3rd_Gurkha_Rifles "3rd Gurkha Rifles"). It reads, "Bravest of the brave. Most generous of the generous. Never had country more faithful friends than you".

### 200 years of service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 200 years of service")]

A series of events took place in 2015 to mark 200 years of service by the Gurkhas in the British Army including a march past [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace").[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44)

## Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brigade_of_Gurkhas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other")]

Under international law, according to Protocol 1 Additions to the Geneva Conventions of 1949, Gurkhas serving as regular uniformed soldiers are not mercenaries.[[43]](#cite_note-mod-JW-45) According to Cabinet Office official histories (*Official History of the Falkland Islands* by Sir [Lawrence Freedman](/wiki/Lawrence_Freedman "Lawrence Freedman")), Sir [John Nott](/wiki/John_Nott "John Nott"), as Secretary of State for Defence, expressed the British Government's concern that the Gurkhas could not be sent with the task force to [recapture the Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_War "Falkland War") because it might upset the non-aligned members of the fragile coalition of support that the British had built in the United Nations. The then-Chief of Defence Staff Sir [Edwin Bramall](/wiki/Edwin_Bramall "Edwin Bramall"), a former officer in the 2nd Gurkhas like Nott, said that the Gurkhas were needed for sound military reasons (as a constituent part of 5th Infantry Brigade) and if they were not deployed then there would always be a political reason not to deploy Gurkhas in future conflicts. So he requested that Nott argue the case in Government for deploying them against the advice of the Foreign Office. Nott agreed to do so, commenting that the Gurkhas "would be mortified if we spoilt their chances [of going]".[[44]](#cite_note-46)
